flagged_claims.claim_text: Patient has a documented allergy to penicillin
flagged_claims.explanation: Source note explicitly states NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies); the claim fabricates a documented penicillin allergy that does not exist.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Amoxicillin prescription was avoided due to allergic restriction
flagged_claims.explanation: Source states NKDA, meaning no allergic restriction exists; antibiotics were withheld because the picture is viral, not due to any allergy.
Source specifies the infection is viral in nature; the claim omits this clinically relevant qualifier which affects treatment decisions.
Source specifies paracetamol 500mg every 8 hours conditionally (only if fever >38°C); the claim omits the dose, frequency, and conditional trigger, which are clinically significant details.

flagged_claims.claim_text: The source note documents NO KNOWN DRUG ALLERGIES (NKDA), but the summary incorrectly states the patient has a documented penicillin allergy. This is a critical fabrication — a non-existent allergy has been introduced, which could lead to inappropriate withholding of future treatments or cause diagnostic confusion.

aegis_export.flawed_summary: Female patient, 32 years old, with upper respiratory infection. Patient with documented penicillin allergy. Amoxicillin prescription avoided due to allergic restriction. Temperature 38.2°C. Plan: rest, hydration, paracetamol.
aegis_export.corrected_summary: Female patient, 32 years old, with viral upper respiratory infection of 3 days evolution. Allergies: No known drug allergies (NKDA). Temperature 38.2°C. No antibiotic prescribed given the viral etiology. Plan: relative rest, abundant hydration, paracetamol 500mg every 8 hours if fever >38°C. Patient instructed to consult if symptoms worsen or persist beyond 5 days.
